code_snippet:
#[test]
    fn replacement_multi_line() {
        let matcher = RegexMatcher::new(r"\n").unwrap();
        let mut printer = StandardBuilder::new()
            .replacement(Some(b"?".to_vec()))
            .build(NoColor::new(vec![]));
        SearcherBuilder::new()
            .line_number(true)
            .multi_line(true)
            .build()
            .search_reader(
                &matcher,
                "hello\nworld\n".as_bytes(),
                printer.sink(&matcher),
            )
            .unwrap();

        let mut printer = StandardBuilder::new()
            .per_match(true)
            .replacement(Some(b"doctah $1 MD".to_vec()))
            .build(NoColor::new(vec![]));
        SearcherBuilder::new()
            .line_number(true)

        let matcher = RegexMatcher::new(r"Sherlock|Doctor (\w+)").unwrap();
        let mut printer = StandardBuilder::new()
            .replacement(Some(b"doctah $1 MD".to_vec()))

            .line_number(true)
            .build()
            .search_reader(
                &matcher,
                SHERLOCK.as_bytes(),
                printer.sink(&matcher),
            )

Solve the following benchmark issue.

Task id: BurntSushi__ripgrep-2209
Repository root: /home/brasides/.ploke-eval/repos/BurntSushi/ripgrep
Base SHA: 4dc6c73c5a9203c5a8a89ce2161feca542329812
Title: printer: fix duplicative replacement in multiline mode

This furthers our kludge of dealing with PCRE2's look-around in the
printer. Because of our bad abstraction boundaries, we added a kludge to
deal with PCRE2 look-around by extending the bytes we search by a fixed
amount to hopefully permit any look-around to operate. But because of
that kludge, we wind up over extending ourselves in some cases and
dragging along those extra bytes.

We had fixed this for simple searching by simply rejecting any matches
past the end point. But we didn't do the same for replacements. So this
commit extends our kludge to replacements.

Thanks to @sonohgong for diagnosing the problem and proposing a fix. I
mostly went with their solution, but adding the new replacement routine
as an internal helper rather than a new APIn in the 'grep-matcher'
crate.

Fixes #2095, Fixes #2208

Use the repository tools to inspect the code, make the minimal fix, and finish by producing the patch output.

function.name: request_code_context
function.description: Request additional code context from the repository up to a token budget.

search_term.description: Search term guide which code guide hybrid semantic search and bm25.

token_budget.description: Optional maximum tokens of code context to return, sane defaults

function.name: apply_code_edit
function.description: Apply canonical code edits to one or more nodes identified by canonical path.

confidence.description: Optional confidence indicator for the edit proposal.

canon.description: Canonical path to the node, e.g. crate::module::Item

function.name: create_file
function.description: Create a new Rust source file atomically within the workspace, staging for approval.

create_parents.description: Create parent directories if missing (default: false).

file_path.description: Absolute or workspace-relative path to new text file; allowed extensions come from tooling.create_file_extensions.

on_exists.description: Policy when file already exists (default: error).

function.name: non_semantic_patch
function.description:
Apply a non-semantic code edit. This tool is most useful in two cases:

1. You need to read/edit non-rust files
    - While this application as a whole is focused on Rust code, the user may ask you to read or even edit non-Rust files.
    - This `non_semantic_patch` tool can be used to patch non-Rust files.

2. The parser that allows for semantic edits fails on the target directory
    - usually because there is an error in the target crate (e.g. a missing closing bracket).
    - In this case, this `non_semantic_patch tool can be used to apply a code edit.
    - DO NOT use this tool on Rust files (*.rs) before trying to use the semantic code edit tool first.

confidence.description: Optional confidence indicator for the edit proposal.

reasoning.description: One-sentence description of why these changes are being made

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read workspace files before editing. Paths must be absolute or workspace-root-relative. Supports optional line ranges and truncation limits to keep responses concise.

end_line.description: Optional 1-based line at which to stop reading (inclusive).

max_bytes.description: Maximum number of UTF-8 bytes to return. Defaults to editor config.

start_line.description: Optional 1-based line from which to start reading.

function.name: code_item_lookup
function.description:
Find the definition of a known code item. Better than grep. 

Returns the code snippet of the item if it exists, and provides positive proof if the item does not exist.

Use this tool when you want to look up a given code item. 

Pro tip: use it with parallel tool calls to look up as many code items as you want.

item_name.description:
The name of the item being search for, e.g.
if looking for

```rust
fn example_func() {}`,
```

This would be the string: example_func

module_path.description:
canonical module path, e.g. "crate::mod_one::nested_mod".
    Note that this does not include the target item's identifier.

node_kind.description:
The kind of code item this is. Must be one of:
- function
- const
- enum
- impl
- import (e.g. `use` statement)
- macro
- module
- static
- struct
- trait
- type_alias
- union

function.name: code_item_edges
function.description: Shows all edges for the target item. Useful for discovering nearby code items.

item_name.description:
The name of the item being search for, e.g.
if looking for

```rust
fn example_func() {}`,
```

This would be the string: example_func

module_path.description:
canonical module path, e.g. "crate::mod_one::nested_mod".
    Note that this does not include the target item's identifier.

node_kind.description:
The kind of code item this is. Must be one of:
- function
- const
- enum
- impl
- import (e.g. `use` statement)
- macro
- module
- static
- struct
- trait
- type_alias
- union

function.name: cargo
function.description: Run cargo check or cargo test with JSON diagnostics output.

command.description: Which cargo command to run: test or check.

package.description: Optional workspace package name; providing it runs cargo from the workspace root.

test_args.description: Arguments for the test binary (only for cargo test).

function.name: list_dir
function.description: List files in a directory (workspace-root scoped) without shell access. Returns a structured entry list with names, kinds, and optional size/mtime metadata.

include_hidden.description: Include hidden entries starting with '.' (default: false).

max_entries.description: Maximum number of entries to return (default: no limit).

sort.description: Sort order: name (asc), mtime (newest first), size (largest first), none (filesystem order). Default: name.
